title: The environment and natural resources from 1968 to 1980
description: Test your skills on the major environmental policies and movements of the 1970s!
topic_path: The environment and natural resources from 1968 to 1980 | 8.13
The environment and natural resources from 1968 to 1980

assessment_items.question_content:
> “As soon as they announced an embargo, one of President Nixon’s top advisers dubbed it ‘an Energy Pearl Harbor.’ There were no bombs, no bloodshed, no loss of life. But the embargo stunned Americans, as if they had come under a surprise attack, if not an outright act of war, because of the serious implications for the economy and the country’s security. By 1973, Americans relied on oil for almost half of all their energy needs, and each day imports made up an expanding portion of the country’s supply. . . . Oil was both the lifeline of the economy and vital resource for the country’s national defense. American oil had played a decisive role in the Allies’ World War II victory, and with the Cold War raging on, U.S. oil remained a top national security concern.”

>-Source: Meg Jacobs, historian, *Panic at the Pump: The Energy Crisis and the Transformation of American Politics in the 1970s,* 2016

**Which of the following was a consequence of the 1973 oil embargo referenced in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: There was a shortage of available gas for automobiles.
The federal government created laws that rationed oil.
The federal government banned domestic production of oil.
There was a quota on immigration because of limited natural resources.

But the embargo stunned Americans, as if they had come under a surprise attack, if not an outright act of war, because of the serious implications for the economy and the country’s security. By 1973, Americans relied on oil for almost half of all their energy needs, and each day imports made up an expanding portion of the country’s supply. . . . Oil was both the lifeline of the economy and vital resource for the country’s national defense. American oil had played a decisive role in the Allies’ World War II victory, and with the Cold War raging on, U.S. oil remained a top national security concern.”

>-Source: Meg Jacobs, historian, *Panic at the Pump: The Energy Crisis and the Transformation of American Politics in the 1970s,* 2016

**Which of the following US actions most directly contributed to the developments described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: supporting Israel in the Middle East
sending troops for the military occupation of Iran
sending financial support to Saudi Arabia and Kuwait
establishing borders between states in the Middle East

assessment_items.question_content:
> “As soon as they announced an embargo, one of President Nixon’s top advisers dubbed it ‘an Energy Pearl Harbor.’ There were no bombs, no bloodshed, no loss of life. But the embargo stunned Americans, as if they had come under a surprise attack, if not an outright act of war, because of the serious implications for the economy and the country’s security. By 1973, Americans relied on oil for almost half of all their energy needs, and each day imports made up an expanding portion of the country’s supply. . . . Oil was both the lifeline of the economy and vital resource for the country’s national defense. American oil had played a decisive role in the Allies’ World War II victory, and with the Cold War raging on, U.S. oil remained a top national security concern.”

>-Source: Meg Jacobs, historian, *Panic at the Pump: The Energy Crisis and the Transformation of American Politics in the 1970s,* 2016

**The conditions described in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a loss in public confidence in the government’s ability to solve economic issues
the mass migration from suburban neighborhoods to condensed urban areas
the reduction in the size of the American military to reduce dependence on oil
a declaration of war on the countries involved in OPEC to end the embargo

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There was no question that in 1974-75, we faced a serious energy problem. Nuclear energy was just beginning to be a factor. Hydroelectric power was at its peak. . . . Oil and gas were having troubles. Domestically, we were so much more dependent on foreign oil. We had a real crisis, so it was important that we make some headway, not only in conservation but in more production.” 

>-Source: Gerald Ford, former president, in an interview with Yanek Mieczkowski, 1994

**The “serious energy problem” referred to in the excerpt was most directly a response to the experience of:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: an embargo by petroleum-producing countries.
an environmental movement advocating for electric cars.
a massive loss of oil-rich domestic lands after increased use.
a series of publicized accidents in coal mines and on oil rigs.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There was no question that in 1974-75, we faced a serious energy problem. Nuclear energy was just beginning to be a factor. Hydroelectric power was at its peak. . . . Oil and gas were having troubles. Domestically, we were so much more dependent on foreign oil. We had a real crisis, so it was important that we make some headway, not only in conservation but in more production.” 

>-Source: Gerald Ford, former president, in an interview with Yanek Mieczkowski, 1994

**The developments described in the excerpt most directly contributed to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: new calls to create a new national energy policy that reduced oil imports
the rise of an environmental movement advocating for the transition to coal
allocation of federal funds to buy back fuel-dependent cars and replace them with electric cars
the development of an Environmental Protection Agency to monitor domestic oil drilling

assessment_items.question_content:
> “There was no question that in 1974-75, we faced a serious energy problem. Nuclear energy was just beginning to be a factor. Hydroelectric power was at its peak. . . . Oil and gas were having troubles. Domestically, we were so much more dependent on foreign oil. We had a real crisis, so it was important that we make some headway, not only in conservation but in more production.” 

>-Source: Gerald Ford, former president, in an interview with Yanek Mieczkowski, 1994

**Which of the following political shifts resulted most directly from the trends described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the development of a federal department to regulate and develop sources of energy
the creation of laws and programs that aimed to protect the environment from poor business practices
the withdrawal of the United States from OPEC in protest of the embargo placed on it
the establishment of federal gas rationing laws to keep the price of gas low during the embargo

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The spill occurred on federal tidelands, under federal leases, with federal permits, and federal approval; therefore, the federal government had full responsibility for creating the conditions that led up to the spill and its aftermath. . . . Understanding the opportunity, Scoop Jackson wasted no time in bringing comprehensive environmental policy to the forefront of discussions with congressional colleagues. . . . The Santa Barbara spill gave the ignored environmental policy champion the perfect weapon to push his ideas through the Senate and to maneuver through House roadblocks.” 

>-Source: Teresa Sabol Spezio, environmental analyst, *Slick Policy,* 2018

**Which of the following earlier trends was most similar to the pattern described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the conservation movement in the 1900s
the Populist movement in the 1890s
the temperance movement in the 1840s
the Romantic movement in the 1830s

assessment_items.question_content:

Understanding the opportunity, Scoop Jackson wasted no time in bringing comprehensive environmental policy to the forefront of discussions with congressional colleagues. . . . The Santa Barbara spill gave the ignored environmental policy champion the perfect weapon to push his ideas through the Senate and to maneuver through House roadblocks.” 

>-Source: Teresa Sabol Spezio, environmental analyst, *Slick Policy,* 2018

**The ideas described in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of public support for government protection of natural resources
the reduction in taxes for companies responsible for oil drilling
the end of oil drilling in the United States in order to protect the environment
the transition to electric and nuclear energy from the use of oil

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The spill occurred on federal tidelands, under federal leases, with federal permits, and federal approval; therefore, the federal government had full responsibility for creating the conditions that led up to the spill and its aftermath. . . . Understanding the opportunity, Scoop Jackson wasted no time in bringing comprehensive environmental policy to the forefront of discussions with congressional colleagues. . . . The Santa Barbara spill gave the ignored environmental policy champion the perfect weapon to push his ideas through the Senate and to maneuver through House roadblocks.” 

>-Source: Teresa Sabol Spezio, environmental analyst, *Slick Policy,* 2018

**The patterns described in the excerpt most directly signaled which of the following later developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the federal government establishing stricter environmental regulations.
the federal government prohibiting any office from leasing land for offshore oil drilling
state and local governments becoming solely responsible for environmental laws
state and local governments can seize control of federal lands

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The disaster was, in fact, no greater than several oil tanker spills the world had suffered but, with the scenic beauty of the Californian coast as a backdrop, it still made for great television. Birds covered with sticky oil struggled for life; dead seals floated ashore; enraged Santa Barbara housewives cried for the cameras. The oil spill’s cost to the environment was immense, but in a sense the tragedy served a purpose, for as the oil spread, so did national outrage.” 

>-Source: J. Brooks Flippen, historian, *Nixon and the Environment,* 2000

**The rise of what the excerpt describes as “national outrage”  most directly contributed to which of the following characteristics of United States politics during the period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of environmentalism as a major policy issue
the demand for acquiring more foreign oil
the decline in political power for oil lobbyists
the emergence of the Green Party as a third party

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: lack of federal regulation of the oil industry
increased demand for oil after an embargo
complaints about the cost of domestic oil
concerns that the US depended too heavily on foreign oil

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of the Environmental Protection Agency
the replacement of oil as the primary energy source for the United States
the prohibition of oil companies drilling off American shores
the expansion of state powers to handle environmental crises
